head.hi: President Wilson to the Secretary of State

div.p:
Papers received. Am I right in supposing that message from Vienna
ibid., p. 436. is merely gratuitous advice from the Austrian Government as to our duty as neutrals?

In regard to the message from Gerard
Foreign Relations, 1915, supp., p. 460. I would suggest if your own judgment coincides that we instruct himFor the instruction, see telegram No. 1918, July 8, 1915, to the Ambassador in Germany,
ibid., p. 462. to convey unofficially to the imperial Government our determination not to yield or compromise in any way our rights as neutrals or prestige of our citizens but our hearty willingness to exercise our good offices with regard to effecting any arrangements which will open the sea to common use with as little danger as possible to non-belligerents, keeping these two things entirely distinct, namely, our rights, which we cannot abate, and our services as friends of all parties. We will discuss anything that it is reasonable and practicable to discuss except the curtailment of our clear and established rights.
